findings.issue: Undefined variable reference. Line 88 executes `aspect_slug <- aspect_slug` when `other_aspect == ""`. At this point, `aspect_slug` has never been defined (it's only assigned in the second branch at line 95-96). This causes an immediate R error: object 'aspect_slug' not found. The function will crash whenever the first branch is taken.
findings.rationale: Variable scope error. The self-assignment is a no-op that relies on prior definition, but the variable only exists in the alternative conditional branch. This is 174 lines of high-leverage munging logic, and a simple path crashes the entire aspect extraction pipeline.
findings.proposed_fix: Initialize `aspect_slug` in the first branch: `aspect_slug <- ""` (line 88) or match the intent of the second branch logic.

findings.issue: Path traversal vulnerability. No validation of `model_path` before `setwd()` (line 73). User can pass `model_path = "../../sensitive_data"` or similar, and the function will change working directory to an arbitrary location without guards. Line 75 strips trailing slashes but does not normalize `..` sequences or validate the path is within expected bounds. This allows directory traversal attacks.
findings.rationale: COUNCIL-SEC principle: input validation gates deserialization. Even though this uses safetensors (safe format), unvalidated path handling is a TRUST_ANCHOR_COMPROMISE because it lets users control where model files are saved. Combined with bert_load which also has no validation, an attacker could save models to system directories.
findings.proposed_fix: Normalize and validate path: (1) normalize: `normalizePath(model_path, mustWork=FALSE)` (2) validate base: `grepl('^[/~]', normalized_path)` (3) disallow `..`: `!grepl('\\.\\.', model_path)`

findings.issue: Same path traversal issue as bert_save. Line 35 `setwd("~")` has no validation of `model_path`. User can pass absolute paths like `/etc/passwd` or relative paths like `../../../sensitive/dir` to bert_load, changing working directory before Python's model loading.
findings.rationale: Mirrored vulnerability. Both bert_load and bert_save use identical unsafe setwd() pattern. This compounds the risk: save to arbitrary location, load from arbitrary location. The safetensors format is safe, but the path handling is not.
findings.proposed_fix: Apply same validation as D002 to bert_load.

findings.issue: Unconditional integer cast on optional parameter. Lines 120-122 guard `max_num_chunks` with `if (length(max_num_chunks) > 0)`, setting it to integer(1) if needed. But line 139 unconditionally casts: `max_num_chunks = as.integer(max_num_chunks)`. When max_num_chunks is NULL, as.integer(NULL) silently returns integer(0), which Python may interpret as invalid or truncate silently, causing unpredictable behavior in chunk handling.
findings.rationale: LANDMINE: RESOURCE_RUNAWAY because incorrect chunk limits can cause memory bloat or chunking to fail. The guard is circumvented by line 139. Python API expects None or integer; integer(0) is neither.
findings.proposed_fix: Respect the guard: either (a) don't cast if length==0, or (b) pass the guarded value: `max_num_chunks = if(length(max_num_chunks) > 0) as.integer(max_num_chunks) else NULL`
findings.old_code_snippet:
if (length(max_num_chunks) > 0) {
      max_num_chunks <- as.integer(max_num_chunks)
    }
    ...
    out <- obj$Top2Vec(
      ...
      max_num_chunks = as.integer(max_num_chunks),

findings.issue: Assumption of named vector not enforced. Line 325 `tibble(id = names(docs), text = docs)` assumes `docs` has names. When docs is an unnamed character vector (the common case), `names(docs)` returns NULL, creating an empty id column. This silently produces malformed output without error.
findings.rationale: FALSE_PREMISE: code assumes docs is always named, but the parameter documentation (L288) does not require names. When docs is unnamed, the output silently has empty ids, which breaks downstream joins and filtering.
findings.proposed_fix: Generate ids if missing: `id = names(docs) %||% glue("doc_{1:length(docs)}")` or validate and error early.

findings.issue: Missing column reference. Line 263 filters by `number_word <= top_n_aspect_words`, but `number_word` is never created in this function (it's only created in `.munge_aspect_column`). When top_n_aspect_words is provided, the filter will fail with column not found error.
findings.rationale: HIDDEN_COUPLING: logic depends on column that doesn't exist in this context. Copy-paste error from aspect_column logic. Breaks when filtering is attempted.
findings.proposed_fix: Either (a) create number_word via row_number(): `mutate(number_word = row_number(), .after = word)` before filtering, or (b) remove the guard (top_n_aspect_words has no effect).
findings.new_code_snippet:
if (length(top_n_aspect_words) > 0) {
      dat <- dat |>
        group_by(topic_bert, label_bertopic) |>
        mutate(number_word = row_number()) |>
        filter(number_word <= top_n_aspect_words) |>
        ungroup()
    }

findings.issue: Silent NULL propagation in comparison. Line 108 `is_reduced_topic = label_bertopic_reduced != label_bertopic` compares two columns, one of which may be entirely NA (when topics_to_merge doesn't match any topics in dat_old). When label_bertopic_reduced is NA, the comparison produces NA (not FALSE), resulting in all NA values in is_reduced_topic. This silently misrepresents which documents had their topics changed, producing unreliable output.
findings.rationale: SINGLE_POINT_DEPENDENCY: the join at line 99-104 is the single point of failure. If no topics match, NAs propagate silently. The caller receives a column full of NAs and may treat them as FALSE (no change), when actually the reduction may have succeeded but the join failed.
findings.proposed_fix: Use coalesce or explicit NA handling: `is_reduced_topic = if_else(is.na(label_bertopic_reduced), FALSE, label_bertopic_reduced != label_bertopic)` or add validation after the join.
findings.old_code_snippet:
dat <-
      dat |>
      left_join(
        dat_old |> distinct(
          topic_bert_reduced = topic_bert,
          label_bertopic_reduced = label_bertopic
        ),
        by = "topic_bert_reduced"
      ) |>
      left_join(dat_old, by = "number_document") |>
      mutate(
        is_reduced_topic = label_bertopic_reduced != label_bertopic,
findings.new_code_snippet:
dat <-
      dat |>
      left_join(
        dat_old |> distinct(
          topic_bert_reduced = topic_bert,
          label_bertopic_reduced = label_bertopic
        ),
        by = "topic_bert_reduced"
      ) |>
      left_join(dat_old, by = "number_document") |>
      mutate(
        is_reduced_topic = if_else(is.na(label_bertopic_reduced), FALSE, label_bertopic_reduced != label_bertopic),

findings.issue: Dead statement: Line 23 `! 'obj' %>% exists() & assign_to_environment`. This expression is evaluated but its result is discarded. It should be `if (...)` guard or removed entirely. The statement has no effect on program flow.
findings.rationale: SIGINT: deprecated purrr pattern + dead code. This mirrors the pattern from Batch A; it's a canary-checked anti-pattern used throughout the codebase as a copy-paste template from before R 4.1+ native pipes.
findings.proposed_fix: Remove the dead line entirely (it's a no-op), or replace with explicit if-guard if intent is conditional assignment.

findings.issue: Dead statement (same as D008). Line 88 `! 'sentence_transformers' %>% exists() & assign_to_environment` is unevaluated code.
findings.rationale: Same canary pattern. Part of systematic dead-code anti-pattern throughout the codebase.

findings.issue: Dead statement (same as D008, D009). Line 136 `! 'transformers' %>% exists() & assign_to_environment`.
findings.rationale: Same systematic pattern across embeddings.R module.

findings.issue: Two instances of dead statement pattern: top2vec.R line 18 `! 'top2vec' %>% exists() & assign_to_environment` and yake.R line 20 `! 'yake' %>% exists() & assign_to_environment`.
findings.rationale: Same systematic dead-code pattern. Part of template reuse throughout the package.
findings.proposed_fix: Remove both dead lines (top2vec.R L18, yake.R L20).
findings.old_code_snippet:
! 'top2vec' %>% exists() & assign_to_environment  // top2vec.R L18
    ! 'yake' %>% exists() & assign_to_environment     // yake.R L20
